hook: You already know how to construct valid logical arguments — you can reach true conclusions from true premises. But a correct proof and a *well-written* proof are two very different things, and the gap between them is what separates a student from a mathematician. How do you make your reasoning so clear and compelling that any reader is immediately convinced?
concept: This unit is about the craft of mathematical communication: turning logically sound ideas into polished, professional proofs that are easy to read, hard to misunderstand, and worthy of peer review — what mathematicians call *mathematical style and exposition*.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Common Proof Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to identify the most frequent logical and stylistic errors in proof writing — such as circular reasoning, missing cases, and unjustified leaps — so you can catch and fix them in your own work.
Master the conventions of mathematical prose — choosing the right level of detail, using precise language, and structuring arguments so a reader can follow every step without confusion.
Apply a systematic revision process to evaluate and improve both your own proofs and those of others, developing the critical eye that professional mathematicians use during peer review.

You will learn strategies for navigating and presenting genuine research mathematics — unpacking dense notation, identifying the core argument, and communicating advanced ideas clearly to an audience.
